aliases: Japanese noodle soup with salt broth

english: Japanese noodle soup with salt broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle dish class; no shio-specific subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class FOODON_03311737.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a shio ramen-specific code. A0FHR (noodle soup) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for shio ramen as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: salt-based broth (chicken or pork bone stock with salt seasoning)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of shio ramen is approximately 500g, comprising noodles (~100g), broth (~300ml), and toppings including chashu pork, egg, fish cake, and bamboo shoots (~100g combined).

nutrient_source.title: Shio Ramen — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Shio Ramen (cr_id F05060114), which provides per-100g values for this specific dish as served in Singapore. Input record confidence is 0.95 with provenance marked canonical.

gi_source.title: Noodle soup dishes — GI analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup (GI ~55) from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shio ramen in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat-based noodle soup dishes, which consistently show GI values in the 47–58 range in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (44g from 500g bowl) / 100 = 24.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 464mg per 100g; a standard 500g bowl delivers approximately 2,320mg sodium, approaching the entire AHA recommended daily limit of 2,300mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the broth and request reduced-salt preparation; consuming noodles and toppings only can reduce sodium intake by approximately 40%, but this dish remains high-risk for regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content at 8.8g per 100g (approximately 44g per 500g bowl) with very low dietary fibre at 0.29g per 100g, which limits glycaemic buffering; estimated GI of 55 places this at the upper boundary of the low glycaemic index range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl, add non-starchy vegetables such as bok choy or spinach to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, and avoid extra noodle servings to keep total carbohydrate intake within meal targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium load (464mg/100g; ~2,320mg per bowl) combined with pork chashu contributing saturated fat; the salt-based broth is a primary driver of cardiovascular risk in this dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently, avoid drinking the broth, choose lean topping options, and be aware that a full bowl may contribute the majority of the recommended daily sodium intake, increasing blood pressure and cardiac workload.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 464mg/100g exceeds the 400mg RA threshold, worsening joint oedema; pork chashu contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced-salt broth, avoid drinking the broth, and add anti-inflammatory toppings such as ginger, seaweed, or leafy greens to partially offset the pro-inflammatory profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shio ramen (塩ラーメン) is a Japanese noodle soup dish featuring wheat-based egg noodles served in a clear, delicate salt-seasoned broth (shio tare) made from chicken or pork bone stock. It is one of the four classic ramen styles in Japan — alongside shoyu, miso, and tonkotsu — and is considered the lightest and most refined in flavour. The dish is typically topped with sliced pork chashu (braised rolled pork belly), a soft-boiled marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), fish cake (narutomaki), and bamboo shoots (menma). As served in Singapore, the dish closely follows the Japanese original. Per 100g, shio ramen provides 92 kcal, 5.79g protein, 3.77g fat, 8.8g carbohydrate, 1.0g sugar, 0.29g dietary fibre, and 464mg sodium. The high sodium content is the primary clinical concern: a standard 500g bowl delivers approximately 2,320mg sodium, approaching the AHA daily limit. The dish has an estimated GI of 55 (upper boundary of low GI range) and provides moderate protein from pork and egg toppings. It is not suitable for patients with hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. For type 2 diabetes, it is manageable in small portions with added vegetables. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal due to pork content.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in shio ramen?
Is shio ramen suitable for hypertension?
What are the calories in shio ramen?
What is the difference between shio and shoyu ramen?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060114) with a canonical provenance flag and 0.95 confidence score. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this shio ramen composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F05060114) as of 2026. Cultural metadata in the input record incorrectly tagged this dish as 'western' cuisine; corrected to 'Japanese' based on dish identity. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values — no direct shio ramen GI measurement exists in approved sources. A GI of 55 is classified as low GI (threshold ≤55 per the University of Sydney GI Database classification). Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (55) × carbohydrate per 500g serving (44g) / 100 = 24.2.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected cuisine from 'western' to 'Japanese'. Added Japanese local name '塩ラーメン'. Added all three required health_context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Populated full ontology section.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is classified as low. Updated gi_source notes and main notes to document this classification. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference 'upper boundary of the low glycaemic index range'.
